does: This paper introduces ActTraitBench, a human-grounded evaluation framework that measures personality consistency in large language models by quantifying the Knowledge-Decision Gap—the disconnect between what LLMs claim about their personalities in explicit self-reports versus how they actually behave in implicit decision-making scenarios. The framework establishes direct mappings between psychometric personality facets and behavioral paradigms derived from empirical human data, then applies Distributional Calibration via Quantile Mapping to align LLM-judge score distributions with actual human norms. Testing across 14 mainstream LLMs reveals that larger, more capable models often exhibit stronger behavioral divergence from their stated personalities, contradicting expectations that scale improves consistency. The paper additionally proposes Chain of Cognitive Alignment (CoCA), a plug-and-play inference method designed to mitigate this gap between stated and demonstrated personality traits.
connects: This work extends research on LLM behavioral consistency and personality modeling, building on prior work examining how well language models can represent stable personas and character traits. It responds to limitations in existing personality benchmarks that suffer from weak construct validity, entangle multiple psychological dimensions, and rely on LLM-based evaluation without human grounding. The paper connects to broader lines of inquiry into LLM faithfulness, value alignment, and the gap between what models claim and what they actually do—issues central to recent work on measuring model honesty and detecting misalignment. It also draws on psychometric theory and behavioral economics paradigms to ground its evaluation methodology in established human personality assessment, moving beyond purely computational metrics toward human-validated behavioral measures.
enables: This framework enables more rigorous auditing of personality consistency in deployed LLMs and provides a methodology for detecting when models present misleading self-representations. The human-grounded approach and distributional calibration technique could be adapted to evaluate other claimed model properties beyond personality—including values, reasoning patterns, or safety commitments. The CoCA inference method points toward broader techniques for aligning stated model behavior with actual behavior, potentially informing work on specification gaming detection and ensuring LLMs reliably behave according to their purported goals. This research supports safer deployment by highlighting which models exhibit problematic gaps between claims and actions, and creates tools for future work examining whether personality consistency training improves both self-reports and behavioral alignment.
